questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a conductor carries no current,
options: the free electrons remain perfectly stationary
the average speed of a free electron over a long time interval is zero
the average velocity of a free electron over a long time interval is zero
the average of the velocities of all free electrons at any given instant evaluates to zero
questions.solution:
In the absence of an external electric field, the free electrons in a conductor are in continuous random thermal motion. Due to this thermal motion, the electrons are never stationary, and their average speed is non-zero.

Because the motion is completely random, the net displacement of any single free electron over a long time interval is zero. Thus, the average velocity of a free electron over a long time interval is zero.

Similarly, at any given instant, the random directions of motion of all the free electrons mean that for every electron moving in one direction, there is another moving in the opposite direction. The vector sum of their velocities is zero, so the average of the velocities of all free electrons at any instant evaluates to zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor without a dielectric is connected to a battery at $t = 0$. Let $A$ be a point in the connecting wires and $B$ be a point between the plates.
options: Current does not flow through $B$.
Current does not flow through $A$.
Current flows through $A$ as long as the charging is incomplete.
Current flows through $B$ as long as the charging is incomplete.
questions.solution:
When a capacitor is connected to a battery, conduction current flows through the connecting wires during the charging process. Since point $A$ lies in the connecting wires, conduction current flows through $A$ as long as the charging is incomplete.

Between the plates of the capacitor, there is no physical medium, so conduction current is zero. However, as the capacitor charges, the electric field between the plates changes with time. This changing electric field produces a displacement current given by $I_d = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$. Since point $B$ lies between the plates, displacement current flows through $B$ as long as the charging is incomplete.

Therefore, current flows through both $A$ and $B$ until the capacitor is fully charged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electrons emitted by a hot filament are accelerated by an electric field, as depicted in the figure. The two stops on the left maintain a uniform cross-section for the electron beam.
options: The speed of the electron is higher at $B$ than at $A$.
The current density is higher at $B$ than at $A$.
The magnitude of the current is greater at $B$ than at $A$.
The direction of the electric current is from left to right.
questions.solution:
The electric field $E$ is directed towards the left.

The force on an electron is $\vec{F} = -e\vec{E}$. Since the electron is negatively charged, the electric force acts in the direction opposite to the electric field, which is towards the right.

This force accelerates the electrons as they move from left to right. Thus, the speed of the electrons increases continuously, making the speed at $B$ higher than at $A$.

In steady state, the current $I$ is constant along the beam. With a uniform cross-sectional area $A$, the current density $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ is also constant.

The conventional electric current flows opposite to the direction of electron motion, meaning it flows from right to left.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the temperature of a conductor rises, its resistivity and conductivity undergo changes. The ratio of its resistivity to its conductivity
options: decreases
increases
may increase or decrease depending on the actual temperature
remains constant
questions.solution:
The conductivity $\sigma$ of a material is the reciprocal of its resistivity $\rho$, so $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$.

The ratio of resistivity to conductivity is given by $\dfrac{\rho}{\sigma} = \dfrac{\rho}{1/\rho} = \rho^2$.

For a conductor, the resistivity $\rho$ increases with an increase in temperature.

Since $\rho$ increases, the square of the resistivity $\rho^2$ also increases.

Therefore, the ratio of resistivity to conductivity increases as the temperature rises.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following quantities does not change when a resistor connected to a battery is heated by the current?
options: Number of free electrons
Resistance
Drift speed
Resistivity
questions.solution:
When a resistor is heated by the current, its temperature increases.

The number of free electrons in a metallic resistor depends on the nature of the material and remains practically constant with changes in temperature.

The relaxation time $\tau$ decreases with an increase in temperature due to more frequent collisions of electrons with the vibrating lattice ions.

Resistivity $\rho = \dfrac{m}{n e^2 \tau}$ increases because $\tau$ decreases.

Resistance $R = \rho \dfrac{L}{A}$ increases as resistivity increases.

Drift speed $v_d = \dfrac{e V \tau}{m L}$ decreases because the relaxation time $\tau$ decreases (assuming the potential difference $V$ from the battery remains constant).

Therefore, only the number of free electrons remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a current flows through a wire having a nonuniform cross-section, which of the following quantities remain independent of the cross-sectional area?
options: The current density
The amount of charge crossing in a given time interval
The free-electron density
The drift speed
questions.solution:
By the principle of conservation of charge, the current $I$ (which is the amount of charge crossing a given cross-section in a given time interval) remains constant throughout the wire in a steady state, regardless of its cross-sectional area.

The free-electron density $n$ is a characteristic property of the material of the wire and is independent of its dimensions.

The current density is given by $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$, which is inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$.

The drift speed is given by $v_d = \dfrac{I}{neA}$, which is also inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$.

Thus, the amount of charge crossing in a given time interval and the free-electron density are independent of the cross-sectional area.

questions.subject: Physics
options: A voltmeter should possess a small resistance.
A voltmeter should possess a large resistance.
An ammeter should possess a small resistance.
An ammeter should possess a large resistance.
questions.solution:
A voltmeter is connected in parallel with the circuit element across which the potential difference is to be measured. To ensure that it draws negligible current and does not alter the actual potential difference, a voltmeter should possess a very large resistance.

An ammeter is connected in series with the circuit to measure the current. To ensure that its inclusion does not significantly increase the total resistance of the circuit and alter the current being measured, an ammeter should possess a very small resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor $C_1$ of capacitance $1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and a capacitor $C_2$ of capacitance $2 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ are charged separately for a long time by a common battery. The two capacitors are subsequently discharged separately through equal resistors. Both of the discharge circuits are connected at $t = 0$.
options: The current in each of the two discharging circuits is zero at $t = 0$
The currents in the two discharging circuits at $t = 0$ are equal but non-zero
The currents in the two discharging circuits at $t = 0$ are unequal
$C_1$ loses $50\%$ of its initial charge sooner than $C_2$ loses $50\%$ of its initial charge
questions.solution:
Let the common battery voltage be $V_0$. The initial voltage across both capacitors is $V_0$.

During discharge, the current at time $t$ is given by $I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} e^{-t/RC}$.

At $t = 0$, the initial current in both circuits is $I(0) = \dfrac{V_0}{R}$. Since $V_0$ and $R$ are the same for both circuits, the initial currents are equal and non-zero.

The charge on a discharging capacitor at time $t$ is $q(t) = q_0 e^{-t/RC}$.

The time $t$ taken to lose $50\%$ of the initial charge is obtained by setting $q(t) = 0.5 q_0$, which gives $t = RC \ln 2$.

Since $C_1 < C_2$, the time constant for the first circuit is smaller ($R C_1 < R C_2$).

Thus, $t_1 < t_2$, meaning $C_1$ loses $50\%$ of its initial charge sooner than $C_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor with a capacitance of $500 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ is connected to a battery via a $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ resistor. Which of the following statements are true regarding the charge stored on the capacitor during the initial $5 \text{ s}$?
options: It is smaller than the charge stored in the subsequent $5000 \text{ s}$.
It is larger than the charge stored in the subsequent $50 \text{ s}$.
It is larger than the charge stored in the subsequent $500 \text{ s}$.
It is larger than the charge stored in the subsequent $5 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The time constant of the $RC$ circuit is given by $\tau = RC$.

Substituting the given values:

$\tau = (10 \times 10^3) \times (500 \times 10^{-6}) = 5 \text{ s}$

The charge on the capacitor as a function of time is $q(t) = q_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, where $q_0$ is the maximum steady-state charge.

The charge stored during the initial $5 \text{ s}$ ($t = \tau$) is:

$q_1 = q(5) - q(0) = q_0(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632 q_0$

The maximum possible charge that can be stored in all subsequent time ($t = 5 \text{ s}$ to $t \to \infty$) is:

$q_{\text{remaining}} = q(\infty) - q(5) = q_0 - 0.632 q_0 = 0.368 q_0$

Since $0.632 q_0 > 0.368 q_0$, the charge stored in the initial $5 \text{ s}$ is strictly greater than the total charge stored in any subsequent time interval, regardless of its duration.

Therefore, the charge stored in the initial $5 \text{ s}$ is larger than the charge stored in the subsequent $5 \text{ s}$, $50 \text{ s}$, $500 \text{ s}$, and $5000 \text{ s}$.
